questions.question: Define stationary distribution of a Markov chain. Find the stationary distribution for the transition matrix P = [[0.7, 0.3], [0.4, 0.6]].
keyPoints: Definition: pi * P = pi with sum(pi_i) = 1
Set up simultaneous equations from pi * P = pi
Use normalization to solve: pi = [4/7, 3/7]
Verify the solution by substituting back

questions.question: What is an absorbing state? Define an absorbing Markov chain. Given a transition matrix with absorbing states, write it in canonical form and find the fundamental matrix N.
keyPoints: Absorbing state: p_ii = 1, chain never leaves once entered
Absorbing chain: at least one absorbing state, all transient states can reach an absorbing state
Canonical form: P = [[I, O], [R, Q]]
Fundamental matrix: N = (I - Q)^(-1)
Interpretation of N: expected visits to transient states before absorption

questions.question: Explain the Fundamental Theorem for Regular Markov Chains. How does regularity guarantee the existence and uniqueness of a stationary distribution? Illustrate with a 3x3 example.
keyPoints: Regular matrix: some power P^k has all positive entries
Theorem guarantees unique pi with all positive entries
P^k converges to matrix with all rows equal to pi
Convergence is independent of initial state
Solve pi * P = pi with normalization for the example

questions.question: Distinguish between regular Markov chains and absorbing Markov chains. Compare their long-term behavior, stationary distributions, and analysis methods.
keyPoints: Regular: no absorbing states, converges to unique stationary distribution
Absorbing: has absorbing states, chain eventually gets trapped
Regular uses pi * P = pi; Absorbing uses fundamental matrix N = (I - Q)^(-1)
Regular: all states visited with positive probability; Absorbing: transient states visited finitely often
Stationary distribution is unique for regular chains, not unique for absorbing chains

questions.question: For the absorbing Markov chain with transition matrix P = [[1, 0, 0], [0.25, 0.5, 0.25], [0, 0.5, 0.5]], identify absorbing and transient states. Find the fundamental matrix, expected number of steps to absorption, and absorption probabilities.
keyPoints: Identify state 1 as absorbing (p_11 = 1), states 2 and 3 as transient
Write canonical form with Q and R submatrices
Compute N = (I - Q)^(-1)
Row sums of N give expected steps to absorption
B = N * R gives absorption probabilities
